critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design with genuine thought given to temporal leakage, class imbalance, and feature isolation. However, I identify three substantive methodological issues, one of which is a significant threat to the core validity claim.

---

**Issue 1: The baseline's sender reputation feature encodes temporal information that the transformer cannot access in the same form — creating an asymmetric information advantage that inverts depending on test-quarter shift.**

The claim: Both models receive 'equivalent metadata,' so any transformer advantage over the baseline reflects learned text understanding.

Why this is wrong: The sender reputation score is defined as 'historical frequency and flagging rate from training data.' This is a precomputed, memorized lookup from quarters 1–12. During test (quarter 16), novel phishing domains — the ones most likely to evade detection and the ones the experiment is supposed to stress-test — will have low historical frequency and no flagging history. Their reputation score will be near zero or undefined, identical to many legitimate new domains. The transformer, operating on raw tokens, can still read the domain name and body text and potentially generalize from linguistic and structural patterns. The baseline, however, has its primary discriminative signal (reputation) muted precisely on the hardest, most novel cases. This means the experiment is not testing 'hand-engineered features vs. learned representations' — it is testing 'stale lookup tables vs. learned representations' on the subset of attacks where stale lookup tables are known to fail. The transformer's advantage on novel campaigns may be entirely attributable to the reputation feature's degradation, not to language understanding. 

What would constitute evidence: (a) Stratify test-set performance by sender domain age in the training set — does the transformer's advantage concentrate in the 'never-seen sender' stratum? (b) Remove sender reputation from the baseline and retrain; if the gap narrows, reputation staleness, not text encoding, explains the delta. (c) Give the baseline a recency-weighted reputation score computed over a rolling window that includes validation-quarter data (consistent with how a production system would actually work); if the gap collapses, the finding is not about text understanding.

---

**Issue 2: The BPE vocabulary is fit on training data, but this is described as preventing leakage — it actually introduces a subtle but real leakage channel through vocabulary coverage statistics.**

The claim: Fitting preprocessing exclusively on training data prevents target leakage.

Why this is wrong: A BPE vocabulary fit on quarters 1–12 will have high coverage for domains, brand names, and lexical patterns prevalent in that period's phishing campaigns. Phishing emails from quarters 13–16 that reuse the same brand impersonation vocabulary will tokenize cleanly; genuinely novel campaigns will fragment into many [UNK]-equivalent subword pieces. This is not leakage in the traditional sense (the test set didn't influence training), but it creates a systematic bias: the transformer is implicitly better at detecting phishing patterns that were already present in training data, and worse at novel patterns — the same novel patterns that would stress-test generalization. This is not a fatal flaw but it means the PR-AUC on quarter 16 is an optimistic estimate for the model's performance on campaigns that are truly out-of-distribution relative to the training vocabulary. The experiment's conclusion that 'the transformer generalizes to novel threats' is overstated if novel threats use novel brand names or attack lexicons.

What would constitute evidence: Measure out-of-vocabulary (OOV) subword rate per email in validation and test sets. Stratify PR-AUC by OOV rate. If performance degrades monotonically with OOV rate, the model is not generalizing linguistically — it is pattern-matching against memorized subwords. A comparison to a character-level or byte-level tokenizer (which has no vocabulary coverage issue) would disambiguate this.

---

**Issue 3: Hyperparameter tuning for XGBoost is done via grid search on the validation set, while the transformer uses early stopping on validation PR-AUC — these are not equivalent tuning protocols, and the asymmetry favors the transformer under certain dataset geometries.**

The claim: 'Equivalent tuning effort' is achieved by grid-searching the baseline and running early stopping for the transformer.

Why this is wrong: Early stopping on validation PR-AUC for the transformer effectively searches over a continuous hyperparameter (number of training steps) that is jointly optimized with the fixed learning rate and architecture. The grid search for XGBoost is over a discrete, finite grid. More importantly, early stopping prevents the transformer from overfitting to the validation set's specific campaign mix, but grid search over depth/learning rate does not give XGBoost the same anti-overfitting protection — depth and regularization control capacity but not the extent to which the model adapts to validation-set artifact patterns across the grid. If the validation set (quarters 13–15) happens to contain a campaign cluster that is absent from the test set, the transformer's early stopping will naturally stop before memorizing it (the PR-AUC will plateau), while the XGBoost grid search may select a configuration that fits it well and then underperforms in quarter 16. This is a subtle asymmetry: early stopping is a stronger regularizer against validation-set overfitting than grid search over capacity hyperparameters.

What would constitute evidence: Report validation PR-AUC vs. test PR-AUC for both models. A large gap (high validation, lower test) for XGBoost but not for the transformer would confirm this asymmetry is operational. Alternatively, apply nested cross-validation over the temporal quarters to give both models equivalent overfitting protection.

---

**Summary:**

Issue 1 is the most serious and the most specific: the sender reputation feature's staleness on novel-domain attacks means the experiment conflates 'transformer beats stale reputation lookup' with 'transformer understands phishing language.' The ablation proposed in Section 7 does not directly address this because it removes reputation from the transformer's input too — the required ablation is removing reputation from the baseline while leaving the transformer intact, and stratifying results by sender novelty. Without this, the headline claim — that learned text representations provide an advantage — is not demonstrated by the data this experiment will produce.
all_issues_raised: Sender reputation is computed from training-era history, making it systematically blind to novel phishing domains — precisely the hard cases the experiment targets — creating an asymmetric information failure that could explain any transformer advantage independently of language understanding.

A BPE vocabulary fit on training quarters will have lower coverage for novel campaign lexicons in the test quarter, meaning the transformer's apparent generalization is partly a vocabulary coverage artifact rather than true linguistic generalization.

Early stopping on validation PR-AUC gives the transformer stronger implicit regularization against validation-set overfitting than grid search over capacity hyperparameters gives XGBoost, making the tuning protocols non-equivalent in a way that favors the transformer.
The feature-restriction design conflates 'raw text access' with 'transformer representational capacity,' requiring a text-augmented GBM baseline to isolate the claimed mechanism.

Refitting a BPE vocabulary on the training corpus and applying it to a pre-trained BERT-like model breaks the pre-trained embedding alignment, a silent misconfiguration that would suppress transformer performance.

Body-last truncation at 512 tokens may systematically eliminate the deceptive payload of long-form spear-phishing emails, the exact attack class where transformer text understanding should have the greatest advantage.

The baseline's text features are a hand-specified projection of the same text the transformer reads in full; the experiment cannot distinguish 'transformer architecture is better' from 'hand-engineered features were incomplete', requiring a full-text GBM condition to resolve.

The sender reputation feature may be computed in a way that is either artificially stale for the baseline or introduces validation-period leakage, creating a calibration asymmetry between models that is uncontrolled.

Fitting a new BPE vocabulary on the training set instead of using the pre-trained model's tokenizer likely destroys the pre-trained embeddings, silently converting the experiment from a test of pre-trained transformers to a test of a randomly initialized transformer — a misconfiguration that produces plausible results while invalidating the core hypothesis.

Bootstrap confidence intervals within a single test quarter produce systematically too-narrow intervals under campaign-level autocorrelation; the stated uncertainty does not reflect across-quarter generalization variance.

issues_raised: The feature-restriction design conflates 'raw text access' with 'transformer representational capacity,' requiring a text-augmented GBM baseline to isolate the claimed mechanism.

Refitting a BPE vocabulary on the training corpus and applying it to a pre-trained BERT-like model breaks the pre-trained embedding alignment, a silent misconfiguration that would suppress transformer performance.

Body-last truncation at 512 tokens may systematically eliminate the deceptive payload of long-form spear-phishing emails, the exact attack class where transformer text understanding should have the greatest advantage.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design with genuinely good practices: temporal splitting, shared metadata between models, PR-AUC as primary metric, bootstrap CIs that respect temporal autocorrelation, and explicit scope limitations. Most objections I could manufacture would be nitpicks. However, I identify three issues with genuine methodological weight.

---

**Issue 1: The baseline's feature-restriction does not isolate 'learned text representations' — it conflates multiple confounds**

The claimed hypothesis is: 'transformer text encoding improves performance over hand-engineered features.' The design operationalizes this by giving the transformer raw text while denying it to the baseline. But this conflates at least two separable factors: (a) access to raw text vs. engineered text features, and (b) the representational capacity of transformers vs. gradient boosting for the *same* inputs. The methodology cannot distinguish whether the transformer wins because neural text representations are richer, or simply because having any access to raw text is better than having keyword-density counts and subject-line length. A proper isolation would require a third condition: GBM trained on bag-of-words or TF-IDF text features (or even just the keyword/body features the transformer uses minus the neural encoding). Without this, if the transformer outperforms the baseline, the gain might be entirely attributable to 'raw text access at all' rather than 'transformer encoding specifically.' The ablation described (transformer with/without metadata) partially addresses this but does not resolve it — you need a text-augmented baseline, not just a metadata-ablated transformer. This is a direct consequence of the experimental design's stated logic about what it isolates.

Evidence for/against: If a GBM with TF-IDF features or a simple logistic regression on bag-of-n-grams achieves near-transformer performance, the representational advantage of transformers is not demonstrated. If it underperforms significantly, the claim is better supported.

---

**Issue 2: The BPE vocabulary is fit on the training set, not on the pre-trained model's existing vocabulary — this breaks the pre-trained representations**

The methodology states: 'tokenized into subword tokens using a BPE vocabulary fit exclusively on the training set.' But distilBERT and RoBERTa have fixed, pre-trained vocabularies that are inseparable from their pre-trained weights. If you retrain the tokenizer on your training corpus and use that new vocabulary, the pre-trained embeddings no longer correspond to the tokens — a token like 'invoice' in your domain-specific BPE may map to a different embedding position than it does in the original model's vocabulary. This is a silent misconfiguration that could degrade transformer performance in a way that produces plausible-looking but suppressed results, making the model appear less capable than it would be with the correct vocabulary. The standard approach is to use the pre-trained model's vocabulary without modification. The 'fit exclusively on training set' language suggests this error may be present.

Evidence for/against: Check whether the tokenizer vocabulary and the pre-trained embedding matrix have the same size. If tokenizer vocabulary ≠ pre-trained embedding vocab, the representations are corrupted. This is empirically testable before any model training.

---

**Issue 3: 512-token truncation combined with body-last truncation may systematically eliminate the signal the transformer is supposed to capture**

The design preserves subject and sender in full, then truncates the body. Phishing emails often embed their primary deceptive content (spoofed branding, fake login links, social engineering language) in the body, not the subject or sender fields. At 512 tokens, after subject, sender domain, recipient list, and [SEP] separators consume 50–150 tokens depending on email metadata length, the body receives 350–460 tokens — roughly 250–330 words. For HTML-stripped text, this may be adequate for commodity phishing but could systematically truncate long-form spear-phishing or business email compromise emails where the social engineering payload appears later in the message. The baseline's keyword-density and body-text-length features, by contrast, operate over the full body. This asymmetry could suppress transformer performance specifically on the attack classes (long-body, targeted phishing) where learning richer representations would be most valuable. It would not produce implausible results — aggregate PR-AUC could still be competitive — but it would mean the experiment does not test transformer capability on its most relevant use case.

Evidence for/against: Report PR-AUC separately for emails where body text exceeds 512 tokens vs. emails where it does not. If the transformer's advantage is concentrated in the short-body stratum and absent or reversed in the long-body stratum, truncation is suppressing the signal the hypothesis targets.

---

**Summary:**

Issue 2 is the most serious: if the BPE vocabulary is truly refit from scratch on the training corpus rather than using the pre-trained model's native tokenizer, it is a silent misconfiguration that corrupts pre-trained representations and potentially invalidates the transformer's measured performance. Issues 1 and 3 are genuine design limitations that restrict the causal claims the study can make. The overall design is otherwise strong.

issues_raised: The baseline's text features are a hand-specified projection of the same text the transformer reads in full; the experiment cannot distinguish 'transformer architecture is better' from 'hand-engineered features were incomplete', requiring a full-text GBM condition to resolve.

The sender reputation feature may be computed in a way that is either artificially stale for the baseline or introduces validation-period leakage, creating a calibration asymmetry between models that is uncontrolled.

Fitting a new BPE vocabulary on the training set instead of using the pre-trained model's tokenizer likely destroys the pre-trained embeddings, silently converting the experiment from a test of pre-trained transformers to a test of a randomly initialized transformer — a misconfiguration that produces plausible results while invalidating the core hypothesis.

Bootstrap confidence intervals within a single test quarter produce systematically too-narrow intervals under campaign-level autocorrelation; the stated uncertainty does not reflect across-quarter generalization variance.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-designed experiment with genuine attention to the most common pitfalls in this domain. The temporal split, the deliberate asymmetry in raw text access, the PR-AUC primary metric, and the temporal robustness check within the test quarter are all correct choices. My critique focuses on three substantive issues that share a common root cause and one that stands alone.

**Root Cause 1: The feature asymmetry is the hypothesis but is not fully controlled**

Issue 1 — The baseline is structurally disadvantaged in a way that conflates 'no raw text' with 'less information.'

The claim: giving the baseline all metadata but no raw text isolates the value of transformer text encoding.

Why this might be wrong: the baseline receives hand-engineered text features (subject length, keyword density, urgency flags), but these are a lossy, human-specified projection of the same text the transformer reads in full. The transformer can recover any keyword-density-style signal the baseline uses, plus arbitrary other signals. The experiment cannot distinguish between (a) the transformer wins because deep text representations are inherently more powerful and (b) the transformer wins because the hand-engineered features are an incomplete projection of text and the specific missing features happen to matter in quarter 16. These are different claims with different implications: (a) would favor transformers broadly; (b) means a better-engineered baseline might close the gap. The ablation described (drop metadata from both models) does not resolve this — it tests metadata contribution, not text-feature completeness.

What would constitute evidence: add a third condition — gradient-boosted model with raw TF-IDF or bag-of-n-grams text features plus metadata — alongside the hand-engineered-only baseline. If this 'full-text GBM' closes the gap substantially, the transformer's advantage is about text access, not architecture. If the transformer still wins substantially, the architecture claim is strengthened.

Issue 2 — The sender reputation feature creates an asymmetric temporal leakage vector.

The claim: the sender reputation score is computed from historical frequency and flagging rate from training data, making it a legitimate feature for both models.

Why this might be wrong: in the production setting, the reputation score at inference time is a rolling feature that updates in near-real-time as new phishing campaigns emerge. If the experiment computes reputation as a static snapshot from training data (quarters 1–12) and applies it frozen to the test set (quarter 16), the feature is artificially stale for the baseline but the transformer can partially compensate by learning sender/domain token patterns from its own parameters — patterns that implicitly encode reputation signals seen during fine-tuning. Conversely, if reputation is re-computed using data from quarters 13–15 (the validation period), it may encode information about campaigns that also appear in quarter 16, producing leakage specifically into the hand-engineered baseline. Either way, the reputation feature introduces a calibration asymmetry between the two models that is not accounted for.

What would constitute evidence: specify and test two reputation-computation regimes — (a) static from training data only, (b) rolling through validation — and report whether the performance gap between models changes between regimes. If the gap is stable, reputation is not the driver. If the baseline improves substantially under regime (b), leakage is likely.

**Root Cause 2: The vocabulary and tokenization choice may silently undermine the transformer's hypothesized mechanism**

Issue 3 — BPE vocabulary fit on the training set conflicts with the use of a pre-trained model.

The claim: tokenization is fit exclusively on the training set to prevent leakage.

Why this might be wrong: the pre-trained BERT-like model (distilBERT, RoBERTa) was trained with a fixed vocabulary and tokenizer. Fine-tuning the model end-to-end does not re-train the embedding table to accommodate a new BPE vocabulary — it continues using the pre-trained vocabulary. If the experiment replaces the pre-trained tokenizer with a training-set-fit BPE tokenizer, the embedding lookup for any token not in the original pre-trained vocabulary will be mapped to [UNK] or randomly initialized embeddings, destroying the pre-trained representations that are the main reason to use a transformer in the first place. The model can still learn, but it degrades to training a transformer from scratch on a small domain corpus, and the reported performance reflects that degraded setting — not the fair evaluation of pre-trained transformers. This is a silent misconfiguration: the model trains and produces plausible outputs, but the hypothesis (pre-trained language representations help) is never actually tested.

What would constitute evidence: confirm that the tokenizer used at runtime is the original pre-trained model's tokenizer, with out-of-vocabulary phishing-specific tokens handled via the standard subword fallback. If a custom vocabulary is used, compare [UNK] rates and downstream PR-AUC between the two tokenization regimes. A large drop in PR-AUC when using the custom vocabulary would confirm the degradation.

**Root Cause 3: Single-quarter test window and the bootstrap confidence interval design**

Issue 4 — One quarter of test data with stratified bootstrap does not adequately characterize variance under campaign-level autocorrelation.

The claim: 95% CIs from 1000 stratified bootstrap resamples over the test quarter capture sampling uncertainty.

Why this might be wrong: the stated concern — that phishing campaigns cluster — is exactly why bootstrap resampling within one quarter is insufficient. If quarter 16 happens to contain a single large campaign that dominates recall, the bootstrap resamples will all include fragments of that campaign and produce narrow confidence intervals that reflect within-campaign variance, not across-campaign or across-quarter variance. The true uncertainty about whether the transformer generalizes to future quarters is not estimable from a single quarter's bootstrap. The temporal robustness check (PR-AUC per week of quarter 16) partially addresses this but still operates within a single quarter. The reported confidence intervals will be systematically too narrow for the claim being made, which is about generalization to future threats.

What would constitute evidence: evaluate on multiple held-out quarters (e.g., treat quarters 13, 14, 15, 16 each as test periods and train on everything prior) and report variance across quarters. If the transformer consistently outperforms across all four periods, the generalization claim is well-supported. If the advantage is concentrated in one period, the single-quarter test result is a lucky draw.
